title: From Answering to Doing: AI Agents
subtitle: When AI stops just talking and starts taking action
theme: Move learners from seeing AI as a clever talker to understanding it as a capable doer — and feel both the excitement and the responsibility that shift brings.
learningObjectives: Explain in plain words how an AI agent is different from a chatbot
Describe what 'tools' are and why giving AI tools changes everything
Walk through how an agent decides its next step, one move at a time
Trace a real example of an agent planning a trip from start to finish
Explain why guardrails and a human-in-the-loop keep agents safe and trustworthy
storyHook: Imagine you ask a friend for restaurant advice and they give you a great answer — then they sit back down. Now imagine a different friend who hears the same question, gets up, books the table, adds it to your calendar, and texts you the time. Same smarts, very different friend. Today we meet that second kind of AI: the one that doesn't just answer, it acts.

slides.title: Welcome: The Day AI Got Up From Its Chair
slides.body:
For the last fourteen days, we've watched AI learn to understand words, see pictures, hear sound, and talk with us like a thoughtful companion. But notice something: in all of that, AI has been a brilliant *talker*. You ask, it answers. You ask again, it answers again. It never gets up.

Today that changes.

Think of the difference between a knowledgeable friend who *tells* you how to fix a leaky tap, versus one who rolls up their sleeves, grabs a wrench, and fixes it. The knowledge is similar. The behavior is completely different. The second friend takes **action** — they do things in the real world, step by step, until the job is done.

That second kind of AI has a name: an **agent**. An agent is an AI that can decide what to do, use tools to actually do it, see what happened, and keep going until your goal is reached.

This is one of the biggest shifts in the whole AI story, and it's why 2026 feels so different from a few years ago. By the end of today, you'll understand exactly how an AI goes from answering to doing.
slides.speakerNotes: Set an excited, slightly mischievous tone — this is the 'AI grows hands' day. Reassure beginners that nothing magical or scary is happening; it's still the same model underneath.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, the assistants people use to book travel, sort their inbox, or draft and send work updates are agents — not just chatbots that wait for the next question.

slides.title: Chatbot vs. Agent: Talker vs. Doer
slides.body:
Let's make the difference crystal clear, because it's the heart of today.

A **chatbot** is a conversation partner. You type, it replies. It's like texting a very smart pen-pal: wonderful advice, zero action. If you ask a chatbot to 'book me a flight,' the best it can do is *describe* how to book one. It can't actually press any buttons.

An **agent** is a chatbot that's been given a body and a to-do list. Same brilliant brain, but now it can *act* — search the web, run a calculation, fill in a form, send a message. And crucially, it works in a **loop**: it does one small thing, looks at the result, decides the next thing, and repeats until your goal is met.

Here's the everyday analogy. A chatbot is a recipe read aloud. An agent is a friend in your kitchen actually cooking the meal — tasting, adjusting, fetching ingredients, plating it up.

So the magic word for today is *loop*. A chatbot does one round-trip. An agent goes around and around, making progress each time, until the work is finished.
slides.speakerNotes: Hammer the 'same brain, new abilities' point so nobody thinks agents are a different, scarier kind of AI. The loop idea is the seed for Day 16.
slides.marketConnection: Tools people met as chatbots in 2023 have quietly grown into agents by 2026 — the same chat box can now actually do the task instead of just explaining it.

slides.title: Giving AI Tools: Hands for the Brain
slides.body:
An agent on its own is still just a thinker. What turns thinking into doing is **tools**.

A tool is simply something the AI is allowed to use to affect the world. Think of them as apps on a phone the AI can tap. The common ones are easy to picture:

- **Search** — looking things up online, like you opening a browser.
- **Code** — writing and running a tiny program to crunch numbers or sort data, like reaching for a calculator.
- **Clicking and typing** — filling forms, pressing buttons on a website, the way you'd navigate a checkout page.

Here's the lovely part: the AI doesn't need to *contain* all knowledge or do everything in its head. It just needs to know *which tool to reach for*. That's exactly how a skilled handyman works — they don't memorize every measurement, they know when to grab the tape measure, the drill, or the level.

Giving a brain hands is the whole leap. A model that can only talk is limited to what it already knows. A model with tools can look things up, test ideas, and change real things in the world. Tools are how an answer becomes an action.
slides.speakerNotes: Emphasize that tools are the bridge between knowing and doing. Keep the phone-apps analogy — beginners find it instantly relatable.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, most serious AI assistants come pre-wired with search, code-running, and the ability to operate other apps, which is why they can finish tasks rather than just suggest them.

slides.title: How an Agent Decides Its Next Step
slides.body:
So an agent has a brain and a toolbox. But how does it know what to do *next*? It doesn't get a script. It figures it out as it goes.

Picture a smart person doing an errand they've never done before. They glance at their goal, look at what they've got so far, pick the single most sensible next move, do it, then look again. *Goal, glance, move. Goal, glance, move.* That rhythm is exactly how an agent thinks.

Each time around the loop, the agent quietly asks itself three things: *What am I trying to achieve? What's the situation right now? What's the one best thing to do next?* Then it uses a tool, sees the result, and asks all three again with fresh information.

The everyday analogy is cooking without a strict recipe. You taste the soup (look at the result), decide it needs salt (choose the next step), add a pinch (use a tool), taste again. Nobody handed you the exact sequence — you sensed your way there, one adjustment at a time.

That's the quiet superpower of an agent: it adapts. If a step fails or surprises it, it simply looks again and picks a new next move.
slides.speakerNotes: Resist any temptation to introduce reasoning chains in depth — that's Day 16. Keep it to the simple 'look, decide, act, look again' rhythm.
slides.marketConnection: When a 2026 assistant hits a sold-out hotel or a broken link, it doesn't give up — it notices, reconsiders, and tries another route, just like a person would.

slides.title: A Trip, Planned End to End
slides.body:
Let's watch an agent actually work. You say: *'Plan me a weekend trip to the mountains under twenty thousand rupees, and put it on my calendar.'*

A chatbot would hand you a tidy list of suggestions and stop. Watch what an agent does instead.

First it **searches** for nearby mountain spots within budget. It finds a few, then searches again for weekend weather — rain ruins one option, so it quietly drops it. Next it checks train and hotel prices, using its **code** tool to add everything up and confirm the total stays under twenty thousand. One hotel is full, so it loops back and picks another. With a plan that fits, it **opens your calendar** and adds the dates, the train times, and the hotel name. Finally, it writes you a short summary: *'Here's your trip, total ₹18,400, all on your calendar.'*

Notice the rhythm — search, check, add up, hit a snag, adjust, book, confirm. That's the loop in action, doing real things in the real world.

The friend who used to *recommend* a restaurant just *booked the table*. That's the moment AI stops being a clever encyclopedia and becomes a genuine assistant.
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through this slowly out loud — it's the emotional centerpiece. Call out each tool use and each 'snag, then adjust' so learners feel the loop.
slides.marketConnection: Travel, shopping, and scheduling assistants in 2026 really do run multi-step jobs like this, handling the boring back-and-forth while you just approve the result.

slides.title: Why This Is a Huge Shift
slides.body:
It's easy to read the trip example and think 'neat trick.' It's much more than that.

For decades, software did *exactly* what it was programmed to do — no more, no less. Every button, every step, had to be spelled out by a human in advance. If a situation came up that nobody planned for, the software just froze or failed.

Agents break that ceiling. You hand over a **goal**, not a step-by-step script, and the AI figures out the steps itself — adjusting on the fly when reality doesn't match the plan. That's the difference between handing someone a paint-by-numbers sheet and simply saying 'paint me a sunset.'

The practical effect is enormous. Whole chores that used to need a human babysitting every click — comparing options, filling forms, chasing down details, stitching results together — can now be handed off as a single sentence. Your time shifts from *doing the steps* to *deciding what you want and checking the result*.

This is why people call agents a turning point, not a feature. We're moving from telling computers exactly *how*, to telling them *what* — and trusting them to work out the rest.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the 'why it matters' beat. Connect it back to Day 1's 'before AI understood us' — software used to be rigid, now it's adaptable.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, more and more work software is sold around 'tell it the outcome you want,' and the rigid click-by-click apps of the past are starting to feel old-fashioned.

slides.title: Guardrails and Keeping a Human in the Loop
slides.body:
A friend who can take action on your behalf is wonderful — and exactly why you'd want some sensible rules. The same is true for agents.

Giving an AI hands means it can now make real changes: spend money, send messages, change files. Most of the time that's the whole point. But you don't want it booking a ₹2,00,000 flight when you said ₹20,000, or emailing your boss without you seeing it first.

So we build **guardrails** — boundaries on what the agent may do alone. Think of it like a learner driver with a calm instructor beside them: the learner does the driving, but for anything risky, the instructor's hand is ready on the wheel. Common guardrails include spending limits, a list of actions that always require a yes from you, and a clear 'stop' button.

That 'yes from you' is the **human in the loop** — the agent pauses at important moments and asks for approval before doing something it can't easily undo. It does the heavy lifting; you keep the final say.

The goal isn't to cage the agent. It's the trust you'd extend to any capable new assistant: let it do plenty, check it on the things that matter, and always be able to step in.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame guardrails as empowering, not fearful — they're what makes it safe to give an agent real responsibility. The learner-driver analogy lands well.
slides.marketConnection: Reputable 2026 agents ask 'shall I go ahead?' before spending money or sending anything on your behalf, and let you set limits and hit stop at any time.

slides.title: Recap: From Talking to Doing
slides.body:
Take a breath — you just watched AI grow a pair of hands. Let's gather what we learned.

A **chatbot** talks; an **agent** acts. Same brilliant brain, but the agent works in a **loop**: do a small thing, see the result, decide the next thing, repeat until the goal is met.

What turns thinking into doing is **tools** — search, code, clicking and typing — the apps the AI can reach for, like a handyman picking the right instrument.

An agent decides its next move by asking, over and over, *what's my goal, where am I now, what's the best next step* — sensing its way forward like a cook tasting and adjusting a soup.

We watched all of this come together as an agent planned a mountain trip end to end, snags and all, and we saw why this is a genuine turning point: we now tell computers *what* we want, not *how* to do it. And we keep it trustworthy with **guardrails** and a **human in the loop**.

Here's the cliffhanger. Our agent today made quick, sensible decisions — but what if a problem is hard enough that the AI needs to slow down and *think carefully* before acting? That's tomorrow.
slides.speakerNotes: Land the recap warmly and confidently, then lean into the Day 16 hook — the loop they learned today becomes the 'thinking loop' tomorrow.
slides.marketConnection: The agents quietly running errands across apps in 2026 are built from exactly these pieces — brain, tools, loop, and guardrails — so you now understand what's under the hood.

keyTakeaways: A chatbot only talks; an agent takes action by working in a loop — do, look, decide, repeat — until your goal is reached.
Tools (search, code, clicking and typing) are what turn an AI's thinking into real-world doing; the AI just needs to know which tool to reach for.
An agent figures out its own next step by repeatedly asking: what's my goal, where am I now, what's the best move next.
Agents let you hand over a goal instead of a step-by-step script — a major shift from rigid, pre-programmed software.
Guardrails (spending limits, approvals, a stop button) and keeping a human in the loop make it safe to give an agent real responsibility.
marketSnapshot2026: By 2026, AI agents have moved from demos to daily life. The assistants people use to manage email, book travel, shop, schedule meetings, and handle routine office work no longer just answer questions — they carry out multi-step tasks across apps and websites, pausing to ask for approval on anything that spends money or can't be undone. The headline change in software this year is the move from 'tell it exactly how' to 'tell it what you want,' with guardrails and human-in-the-loop approval now treated as standard, expected features rather than extras.
homework: Pick one small, real errand you do online — comparing two products, finding a good local restaurant, or planning a short outing. On paper, write down the goal in one sentence, then list the step-by-step moves you'd take and which 'tool' each step uses (search, calculator, a website button). Finally, circle the one or two steps where you'd want an AI to stop and ask your permission before acting. You've just designed your first agent — guardrails and all.
nextDayTeaser: Today our agent made quick, on-the-fly decisions. But some problems are too tricky for snap judgments — they need the AI to slow down, think things through, and even catch its own mistakes before acting. Tomorrow, in Day 16: Thinking in Loops: Reasoning Models, we'll meet AI that pauses to reason carefully, turning today's action loop into a genuine thinking loop.
